critique.summary: The article announces the open source release of Edge.js, a JavaScript runtime designed to run Node.js applications safely inside a WebAssembly sandbox. Edge.js aims to provide full Node.js compatibility with improved security and startup times compared to container-based solutions, leveraging WebAssembly and WASIX for sandboxing unsafe system calls and native modules.
mainPoints: Edge.js allows running unmodified Node.js applications and native modules safely sandboxed using WebAssembly and WASIX.
It preserves full Node.js compatibility (targeting Node.js v24) and uses the same dependencies as Node.js for consistent behavior.
Edge.js runs JavaScript engines natively and isolates only unsafe parts like system calls, improving performance over running V8 inside WebAssembly.
The runtime supports major Node.js frameworks such as Next.js and Astro without modification and commits to fixing compatibility bugs quickly.
Performance is currently about 5-20% slower than native Node.js but with plans to improve startup times and runtime speed.
Edge.js was developed with significant assistance from AI tools like GPT-5.4 and OpenAI Codex, accelerating development.
The project aims to offer a more compatible and efficient alternative to other edge runtimes like Deno, Bun, and WinterCG, which have compatibility or fragmentation issues.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are largely accurate based on available information about Edge.js and Wasmer's technology. Wasmer is a known company specializing in WebAssembly runtimes, and the approach of sandboxing Node.js workloads using WebAssembly and WASIX is technically plausible and aligns with current trends in secure, serverless computing. The compatibility claims are supported by detailed test results comparing Edge.js with other runtimes, which appear consistent with known limitations of those runtimes. The performance figures and architectural explanations are reasonable and reflect the trade-offs inherent in sandboxing and native engine integration. The use of AI tools for development acceleration is plausible but anecdotal and not independently verifiable. Overall, the article provides a credible overview of Edge.js with technical depth and transparency about current limitations and future plans.
considerations: The article is authored by the founder and CEO of Wasmer, which may introduce bias towards promoting their product.
Independent third-party benchmarks and reviews would be useful to confirm performance and compatibility claims.
The security of the sandbox depends heavily on the robustness of WASIX and WebAssembly isolation, which should be audited by external experts.
The article does not discuss potential limitations or challenges in migrating existing Node.js applications to Edge.js beyond compatibility bugs.
The impact of running native modules and system calls in a sandbox on application behavior and debugging is not deeply explored.
The reliance on AI tools for development, while innovative, raises questions about maintainability and code quality that are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the new Edge.js project, which runs Node.js apps inside a WebAssembly sandbox. Commenters are curious about use cases, platform compatibility, and sandboxing capabilities, with some expressing enthusiasm and others seeking clarification on practical applications.

comments.author: syrusakbary
comments.text:
Hi HN!

I'm Syrus, from Wasmer. We built Edge.js in a few weeks after different trials trying to bring Node.js to the Edge. We used AI and Codex heavily for this project, as otherwise the timeline would have spanned to a year plus to develop.

The summary of this announcement is that Edge.js:

  * Runs using WebAssembly when in `--safe` mode
  * It's fully compatible with Node.js (passing all their spec tests for non-VM modules)
  * It has a pluggable JS engine architecture: can work with V8, Javascript, SpiderMonkey, QuickJS, Hermes, etc.
Super happy to answer any questions you may have!
comments.analysis: As the project creator, this comment provides authoritative and accurate information about Edge.js features and development background.

comments.author: pacman1337
comments.text:
Not sure I understand the use cases. I'm guessing people want to run unsafe nodejs code. Either cloud providers like for lambdas or on personal computer for AI coding? On cloud why is this better than firecracker, firecrack can run any programming language this is just nodejs so already useless for that use case?

For personal computer, people worried about the spin up time of docker? I think that is more of a tooling issue where you spin up one instance and run multiple jobs.

What am I missing? What are actually real use cases where this would be better?
comments.analysis: Raises valid questions about the practical advantages and target use cases of Edge.js compared to existing sandboxing solutions like Firecracker.

comments.author: alex_reg
comments.text:
It's a bit confusing.

Roughly:

* a refactor of Node.js, but using a standardized API for JS engine interop * Integration with the Wasmer CLI so it will run JS with v8 but, everything else in Webassembly

Interesting idea.

Could be a much lighter weight way to sandbox JS...
comments.analysis: Provides a reasonable summary and interpretation of the project, helping clarify its architecture and potential benefits.

comments.author: robjam
comments.text:
Wow! Node APIs in wasm(wasix)? Something that I have been thinking over for a long time is getting wasm (just wasm, not wasi or com) to be the unit of deployment/packaging for web apps like Nuxt/Next that only depend on, for example SQLite or Postgres. Slightly ignorant question, but would edge.js allow integrating with a caddy plug-in to handle serving the app from wasm?

I know of the extism project and played around with it in elixir, but I'm looking to have CF Workers DX with as little operational complexity as possible for many silly pet projects.
comments.analysis: Explores advanced deployment scenarios and integration possibilities, showing understanding of wasm and serverless environments.

comments.author: 2001zhaozhao
comments.text: Huh. Could this be a way to sandbox user-generated JS in web apps?
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant question about sandboxing user-generated code, reflecting a common use case for such technology.

comments.author: pscanf
comments.text:
Very cool project!

Question regarding the pluggable js engine: I have an electron app where I'm currently using QuickJS to run LLM-generated code. Would edge.js be able (theoretically) to use electron's v8 to get a "sanboxed within electron" execution environment?
comments.analysis: Asks a specific technical question about compatibility and sandboxing within Electron, showing practical interest in the technology.

comments.author: MillionOClock
comments.text: Very interesting! On what platforms can this run? If it can run on iOS, how would you handle attempts to access to the file system or networking, is this already wired in somehow? If not is it easy to add custom handlers to handle these actions?
comments.analysis: Raises important questions about platform support and security controls, which are critical for sandboxed environments.

comments.author: willquack
comments.text:
Awesome project!

Dumb question: could you run this in frontend js using the browser's js engine and wasm environment similar to WebContainers? Maybe `fs` is just in-memory, and some things like forking are disabled. It'd be cool to have "nodejs" in the web!
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm and asks about browser-based use cases, which is a natural extension but may have technical limitations.

comments.author: actionfromafar
comments.text:
Can that Node app load and run WebAssembly?

(A.K.A. are we IBM 360 yet?)
comments.analysis: Briefly inquires about WebAssembly support within Node apps running on Edge.js, a relevant but vague question.
